title: SEGMENT 3 — The basic recipe: stem cells, gel, cocktail, time

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The basic recipe: stem cells, gel, cocktail, time
Let me pause for a moment and walk through, in slightly more concrete terms, what's actually in an organoid dish.
Because this is the kind of thing where it's easy to talk in abstractions, and I want you to have a physical mental picture of what's happening on the bench.
You start with stem cells.
The source matters, because different organoids use different starting points.

For intestinal organoids and most other adult-tissue organoids, you use adult tissue stem cells — cells dissociated from a piece of biopsy or surgical specimen, with the stem cells of interest enriched either by sorting for a surface marker (like Lgr5) or just by trusting that the conditions will favor stem cell outgrowth.

For cerebral organoids and a lot of the developmental work, you use pluripotent stem cells — embryonic stem cells (ES cells) or, more commonly today, induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) generated by reprogramming someone's skin or blood cells back to a pluripotent state.
The choice of cell source determines a lot about what the organoid can be.
Adult stem cells give you the adult tissue.
Pluripotent cells let you start from scratch and push the system down a particular developmental lineage.
You take those cells — usually as a single-cell suspension, or as small clumps — and you mix them with Matrigel that's still in its liquid state, on ice.
You pipette droplets of this cold mixture onto a culture dish.
As the gel warms to body temperature, it solidifies, encapsulating the cells in three-dimensional protein scaffolding.
You now have islands of cells suspended in jelly.
Then you flood the dish with culture medium containing your cocktail of growth factors and small molecules, and you put the whole thing in a 37-degree incubator at 5% carbon dioxide.
That's it.
That's the setup.
The Matrigel piece deserves a closer look, because it's the unsung hero of most organoid protocols.
Matrigel comes from a tumor called the Engelbreth-Holm-Swarm sarcoma, which secretes an unusual amount of basement membrane material.

When you process the tumor, you get a soup of laminin, collagen IV, entactin, perlecan, and a bunch of bound growth factors — exactly the kind of protein cocktail that, in a real animal, sits underneath every epithelium and provides the substrate that those epithelial cells adhere to.
Matrigel is, in effect, the scaffolding the cells climb on — a soup of basement-membrane proteins that gives them a three-dimensional structure to build around.
The reason Matrigel works so well is that real cells in real tissues use these very proteins as their attachment substrate.
The integrin receptors on the cell surface have evolved to grab laminin and collagen IV.
When you put a cell in Matrigel, those receptors find their natural ligands, and the cell experiences the chemical environment it would experience in vivo.
It can polarize properly — meaning, develop a "top" (the apical surface, facing a lumen) and a "bottom" (the basal surface, attached to the basement membrane).
Polarity is the prerequisite for all epithelial organization.
Without polarity, you have a clump.
With polarity, you have the makings of an epithelium.
Now, Matrigel has problems.
It's mouse-derived, so it's lot-to-lot variable and not appropriate for clinical applications.
The growth factor content varies.
It's a defined-but-not-fully-defined system.

A lot of the recent engineering effort in the field has gone into developing synthetic alternatives — chemically defined hydrogels made from synthetic peptides or modified polysaccharides that mimic the relevant features of Matrigel without its variability.
Those alternatives are improving but aren't yet as universally good as Matrigel itself.
So for now, Matrigel remains the workhorse.
The growth factor cocktail is the other half of the recipe, and it's where most of the variation between organoid types lies.
Think of the cocktail as the conductor's score — each growth factor tells the cells what to become, when, and in what order.
Wnt tells cells to stay stem-like.
R-spondin amplifies that signal in cells expressing Lgr5.
Noggin blocks BMP and prevents premature differentiation.
EGF promotes proliferation.
FGF, in various flavors, is used in lung and liver and kidney organoids to drive different aspects of organ development.
SHH — sonic hedgehog — is used in cerebral organoids to ventralize the tissue.
RA — retinoic acid — is used to specify anterior-posterior axis identity.
The list is long, and every organ has its own cocktail.
What's important to understand is that this cocktail is not random.
It's reverse-engineered from developmental biology.
We know, from decades of work in mice and frogs and zebrafish, which signals the embryo uses to specify each organ.
The organoid cocktail is, essentially, a synthetic recapitulation of those embryonic signals.
The cells in the dish are being told, through the soluble factors in the medium, "you're in the intestine now" or "you're in the developing forebrain now" or "you're in the early liver bud now.
" And the cells, recognizing these cues from their evolutionary playbook, do what they would have done in that location in a real embryo.
This is why I sometimes describe organoids as developmental biology in a dish.
We're not just culturing cells; we're rerunning a portion of embryonic development, in vitro, with the embryo's own signals reconstituted from purified proteins.
The cells don't know they're in a dish.
They think they're in an embryo.
And they do what cells in an embryo do.
A few more practical notes.

The medium is typically serum-free — most organoid systems have moved away from fetal calf serum, which is variable and contains uncharacterized factors, toward fully defined media supplemented with the specific growth factors needed.
This matters because reproducibility is hard enough without random batch-to-batch variation in serum.

Organoids are typically passaged every one to two weeks — meaning, you take the established organoids, break them up mechanically or with enzymes into smaller fragments or single cells, and replate those in fresh Matrigel.
Many organoid lines can be passaged for months or years, accumulating few mutations and maintaining their tissue identity.
Some — particularly tumor organoids — are essentially immortal lines that can be passaged indefinitely.
And they can be frozen.
You can take a successful organoid line, dissociate it, freeze it down in liquid nitrogen, and thaw it years later.

This is what makes biobanks possible — we'll talk about the Hubrecht's enormous tumor organoid biobank in a later segment — and it's what makes organoids practical as a research resource rather than a precious one-off.
That's the basic recipe.
Now let's see what people have grown with it.
